perturbation.original_text: C onfidential Information does not include information which a party can demonstrate and document that: (a ) was in his or her knowledge or possession prior to receiving the information from the other party; or, (b) was public knowledge or has become public knowledge through no fault of the party claiming that the information is not Confidential Information; or, (c) was independently developed without reference to the Confidential Information; or, (d) came into his or her possession through a third party who was not obligated to keep the information confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Protected Data does not include information which a party can demonstrate and document that: (a ) was in his or her knowledge or possession prior to receiving the information from the other party; or, (b) was public knowledge or has become public knowledge through no fault of the party claiming that the information is not Confidential Information; or, (c) was independently developed without reference to the Confidential Information; or, (d) came into his or her possession through a third party who was not obligated to keep the information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Protected Data' in the first sentence of Section 2, but then the rest of the paragraph continues to use 'Confidential Information'. This creates an in-text contradiction because it is unclear whether the exceptions apply only to 'Protected Data', or to 'Confidential Information', or to both. The perturbation introduces ambiguity as to whether 'Protected Data' is intended to be synonymous with, a subset of, or something entirely distinct from 'Confidential Information,' as defined elsewhere in the document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C onfidential Information does not include information which a party can demonstrate and document that: (a ) was in his or her knowledge or possession prior to receiving the information from the other party; or, (b) was public knowledge or has become public knowledge through no fault of the party claiming that the information is not Confidential Information; or, (c) was independently developed without reference to the Confidential Information; or, (d) came into his or her possession through a third party who was not obligated to keep the information confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text and contradicted text are identical. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Data' in the introductory sentence, but the rest of the paragraph continues to refer to 'Confidential Information'. This creates a contradiction because it becomes unclear to which term the exceptions (a, b, c, d) apply. The explanation provided clearly outlines that this is a case of misaligned terminology creating an in-text contradiction as the contract now uses two terms without defining their relationship.

perturbation.original_text: The parties shall: (a ) not disclose any Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without the prior written permission of the other party; and, (b) not take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without the written permission of theother party; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall: (a ) not disclose any Sensitive Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without the prior written permission of the other party; and, (b) not take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without the written permission of theother party; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 3, 'Confidential Information' is changed to 'Sensitive Information' in subsection (a), but the rest of the section continues to use 'Confidential Information.' This creates an in-text contradiction about whether the restrictions on disclosure apply to all 'Confidential Information' or just the subset deemed 'Sensitive Information.' It's never indicated the difference or relation between 'Sensitive Information' and 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties shall: (a ) not disclose any Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without the prior written permission of the other party; and, (b) not take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without the written permission of theother party; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' in subsection (a), while the rest of the section and the contradicted text continue to use 'Confidential Information.' This creates a direct conflict and ambiguity as to which type of information is subject to the disclosure restrictions, leading to a strong in-text contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports the classification of 'YES' as it directly aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology, triggering a direct and easily identifiable conflict according to the guidelines provided.

perturbation.original_text: Notwiths tanding the duty of non-disclosure in paragraph 3, the parties may disclose Confidential Information to their directors, officers, employees, advisors and representatives, provided that these persons are bound by the duty of non-disclosure in this agreement and are informed of this duty.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwiths tanding the duty of non-disclosure in paragraph 3, the parties may disclose Protected Information to their directors, officers, employees, advisors and representatives, provided that these persons are bound by the duty of non-disclosure in this agreement and are informed of this duty.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Information' in Section 4 introduces an in-text contradiction. It's now unclear if the parties can disclose all types of 'Confidential Information' or only the subset referred to as 'Protected Information'. Because Protected Information has not be defined in the contract, it leads to a potential legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwiths tanding the duty of non-disclosure in paragraph 3, the parties may disclose Confidential Information to their directors, officers, employees, advisors and representatives, provided that these persons are bound by the duty of non-disclosure in this agreement and are informed of this duty.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Information' in Section 4. This directly contradicts the original meaning and creates an in-text contradiction, especially since 'Protected Information' is not defined. The contradicted text refers to 'Confidential Information,' and the change introduces ambiguity and potential conflict in interpretation because it's unclear whether the parties can disclose all types of 'Confidential Information' or only the subset referred to as 'Protected Information'. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition: 'Misaligned terminology also leads to in-text contradictions when the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.'

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall terminate upon the mutual agreement of the parties, or at such time that the parties and their assigns no longer have any legal or equitable claim to anyConfidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall terminate upon the mutual agreement of the parties, or at such time that the parties and their assigns no longer have any legal or equitable claim to any Private Data.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 6, the term 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Private Data'. The term 'Private Data' is never defined in the document. Now, it's unclear whether the agreement terminates only when there are no longer claims to 'Private Data' or if 'Confidential Information' still matters for termination. This creates potential for a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This agreement shall terminate upon the mutual agreement of the parties, or at such time that the parties and their assigns no longer have any legal or equitable claim to anyConfidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text and contradicted text are the same. The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Private Data', which is undefined. This introduces ambiguity because it is unclear whether the agreement terminates only when there are no longer claims to 'Private Data' or if 'Confidential Information' still matters. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition, causing an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS , unauthorized disclosure of any confidential information to any person or entity will cause irreparable harm to the parties
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS , unauthorized disclosure of any secret information to any person or entity will cause irreparable harm to the parties
perturbation.explanation: In the preamble, the term 'confidential information' is replaced with 'secret information'. This creates ambiguity because the agreement consistently refers to 'Confidential Information' throughout, and the switch to 'secret information' introduces uncertainty about the scope of the WHEREAS clause. Therefore, the parties' intent to exchange and protect information is undermined by this switch, leading to legal vulnerabilities. The definition of Confidential Information is key to the contract's enforcement; changing the qualifier of the harm caused creates uncertainty, and legal risk for the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C onfidential Information means any information not generally known to the public, whether recorded or conveyed in written, graphic, oral or physical form, including but not limited to scientific knowledge, know-how, processes, inventions, techniques, formulae,products, business operations, customer requirements, data, original copyrighted orcopyrightable works, plans or other records, biological materials, and/or software.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'confidential information' to 'secret information' in the preamble introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of information protected, which contradicts the detailed definition of 'Confidential Information' elsewhere in the document. This misalignment of terminology, as defined in the perturbation explanation, creates uncertainty and potential legal vulnerabilities, making it a strong in-text contradiction.
